post.title: No Vibe Debugging: Running Agents in Production

post.content:
No Vibe Debugging: Running Agents in Production

The demo looks flawless. Your AI agent handles customer queries with human-like precision, routes complex requests seamlessly, and even cracks the occasional joke. The board is impressed. The engineering team is confident. Then you flip the switch to production.

Within hours, the agent starts hallucinating product features that don't exist. It confidently tells customers their orders shipped when they're still in processing. It escalates routine questions to human agents while auto-resolving genuine emergencies. The "intelligent" system you built suddenly feels like a black box making expensive mistakes at scale.

This is the gap between vibe-driven development and production-grade agent operations. Building an agent that works in controlled conditions is table stakes. Running one that performs reliably under real-world chaos requires a fundamentally different approach.

Why now

Two shifts make this problem urgent. First, the cost of agent mistakes has moved from "embarrassing" to "business-critical." When agents handle customer support, sales qualification, or financial transactions, a hallucination isn't just a bad user experience—it's a compliance violation, a lost deal, or a damaged relationship.

Second, the tooling gap is closing. The same observability and reliability patterns that transformed microservices deployment are now available for agent workloads. Teams can finally instrument, monitor, and govern AI systems with the same rigor they apply to traditional software.

What we mean by "agent operations"

Agent operations is the discipline of running AI agents reliably in production environments. It encompasses monitoring agent behavior, governing decision-making processes, and maintaining performance under varying conditions—all while preserving the autonomous capabilities that make agents valuable.

Unlike traditional software that follows deterministic code paths, agents make probabilistic decisions based on context, training, and real-time inputs. This fundamental difference requires new operational patterns that account for uncertainty while maintaining predictable business outcomes.

How we got here

The evolution from chatbots to production agents mirrors the broader shift from scripts to microservices. Early chatbots followed rigid decision trees—predictable but limited. Large language models unlocked natural conversation but introduced unpredictability.

The first wave of AI applications treated models like APIs: send a prompt, get a response, hope for the best. Teams built elaborate prompt engineering workflows and crossed their fingers. This worked for demos and low-stakes use cases but broke down under production load.

The current wave recognizes that agents are systems, not just models. They require the same operational discipline as any distributed system: monitoring, alerting, circuit breakers, and graceful degradation. The difference is that instead of tracking CPU and memory, you're tracking reasoning quality and decision accuracy.

What it actually does

Agent operations starts with behavioral monitoring—tracking not just whether the agent responds, but whether it responds correctly. This means instrumenting decision points, logging reasoning chains, and measuring outcomes against business objectives.

The system observes agent interactions in real-time, flagging anomalies like sudden changes in response patterns, unexpected escalation rates, or deviations from established workflows. When an agent starts making questionable decisions, operators can intervene before customers notice.

Governance layers enforce guardrails without breaking agent autonomy. Agents can still reason and adapt, but within defined boundaries. A customer service agent might have full latitude to resolve billing questions but require human approval for refunds over a certain threshold.

Vibes vs operations — the real shift

Vibe-driven development relies on subjective assessment: "The agent feels smart," "The responses seem helpful," "It's probably working fine." Teams test with cherry-picked examples and deploy based on gut feeling. This approach scales poorly and fails unpredictably.

Operations-driven development measures objective outcomes: response accuracy, task completion rates, escalation patterns, and business impact. Teams instrument every decision point and deploy based on data. When something breaks, they know exactly what went wrong and how to fix it.

The shift isn't about removing human judgment—it's about augmenting it with systematic observation. Instead of guessing whether an agent is performing well, operators know precisely how it's behaving and why.

What good looks like

Production-ready agent operations exhibits four characteristics: observability, governance, reliability, and adaptability.

Observability means every agent decision is logged, traced, and measurable. Operators can reconstruct any interaction, understand the reasoning process, and identify improvement opportunities. Dashboards show real-time performance metrics alongside business outcomes.

Governance provides guardrails without micromanagement. Agents operate within defined policies but retain decision-making autonomy. When they encounter edge cases or high-stakes scenarios, they escalate appropriately rather than guessing.

Reliability ensures consistent performance under varying conditions. Agents degrade gracefully when facing unexpected inputs, maintain response quality during traffic spikes, and recover quickly from failures.

Adaptability allows continuous improvement without system downtime. Teams can update agent behavior, refine decision logic, and deploy improvements incrementally while maintaining service availability.

How to adopt without boiling the ocean

Start with instrumentation—add logging and monitoring to existing agent workflows before changing any core functionality. Identify the key decision points where agents choose between different actions and ensure those choices are observable.

Next, establish baseline metrics for current performance. Measure response accuracy, task completion rates, and user satisfaction scores. These baselines become the foundation for improvement and the safety net for future changes.

Finally, implement progressive governance—start with monitoring and alerting, then gradually add automated guardrails as you understand agent behavior patterns. This approach builds confidence while minimizing disruption to working systems.

Roles you'll need

Agent engineers build and maintain the core agent logic, focusing on reasoning capabilities and decision-making processes. They understand both the business domain and the technical constraints of AI systems.

Agent operators monitor day-to-day performance, respond to incidents, and tune system behavior based on observed patterns. They bridge the gap between engineering and business teams, translating technical metrics into business impact.

Anti-patterns to avoid

Prompt perfectionism assumes that better prompts solve operational problems. While prompt quality matters, production issues usually stem from systemic failures rather than individual response quality. Over-automation removes human oversight from high-stakes decisions, creating risk without proportional benefit. Metric gaming optimizes for easily measured outcomes while ignoring harder-to-quantify business value. Black box deployment treats agents as unchangeable systems rather than continuously improvable software. Scale-first thinking prioritizes handling more requests over handling requests better.

Build vs buy

Build custom agent operations when your use case requires domain-specific reasoning, tight integration with existing systems, or novel operational patterns. The investment pays off when agent performance directly impacts core business metrics and you have the engineering resources to maintain specialized tooling.

Buy existing solutions when your operational needs align with standard patterns: customer support, content generation, or data processing. Commercial platforms provide proven reliability and faster time-to-value, especially for teams without deep AI operations expertise.

The decision often splits along organizational lines: companies with strong platform engineering teams tend to build, while those focused on business applications tend to buy.

The bigger shift

Agent operations represents a fundamental change in how we think about AI systems. Instead of treating them as magical black boxes that either work or don't, we're applying the same engineering discipline that made cloud computing reliable and scalable.

This shift unlocks AI's true potential: not just impressive demos, but dependable systems that businesses can bet on. When agents become as reliable as any other production service, they stop being experimental tools and become essential infrastructure.
